FRACTIONAL CMO · COMPARISON

Fractional CMO vs interim CMO.

Same hourly rate, sometimes. Very different intent. A fractional CMO is built to last and create compounding systems over a 6–18 month engagement. An interim CMO is built to bridge a 90–180 day gap while you search for a permanent replacement. Picking the wrong one is the cheaper false economy.

The TL;DR

The honest one-paragraph answer

If your previous CMO just left and you're actively searching for the permanent replacement, hire an interim CMO — full-time, expensive ($20K-$40K/month), designed to keep things running. If you're building the marketing function for the first time or you want senior leadership as an ongoing operating model (not a placeholder), hire a fractional CMO — part-time, half the monthly cost, designed to build something durable.

What each is for

Different roles for different problems

Interim CMO

  • Time: Full-time, 5 days per week.
  • Duration: 90-180 days typical; ends when permanent CMO starts.
  • Cost: $20K-$40K/month all-in.
  • Primary job: Stability. Keep the existing function running. Maintain team morale. Manage existing vendor relationships. Don't break anything.
  • Secondary job: Sometimes participate in CMO search (less common — usually CEO + search firm own this).
  • Typical context: Previous CMO departed; team has 6-15 people; the company is mid-flight on launches and campaigns that need senior coverage.

Fractional CMO

  • Time: Part-time, 1-3 days per week.
  • Duration: 6-18 months typical; ends when the function is durable or when a full-time CMO replaces them.
  • Cost: $8K-$25K/month all-in.
  • Primary job: Build. Set strategy, install systems, develop team, define KPIs, get the function producing.
  • Secondary job: Often help recruit the eventual full-time hire (themselves or another).
  • Typical context: No senior marketing leader currently in place; company is building marketing function for the first time or rebooting it.
Side-by-side

Direct comparison

DimensionInterim CMOFractional CMO
Time commitmentFull-time (5 days/week)Part-time (1-3 days/week)
Monthly cost$20K-$40K$8K-$25K
Typical engagement90-180 days6-18 months
Primary intentStabilize during transitionBuild a durable function
Builds new systemsRarely (don't disrupt)Yes — primary deliverable
Team leadershipYes — for existing teamYes — builds or develops team
Brand + positioning workRareCommon
Board reportingYesYes
Exit transitionTo permanent CMOTo full-time CMO or self-running function
Best fit company stageEstablished function, departureBuilding function, no senior leader
Common confusion

Why founders mix these up

The market uses these terms loosely. Some "fractional CMO" providers will pitch interim work; some "interim CMO" search firms will offer fractional packaging. The labels matter less than the intent — but if you're buying the wrong intent, you'll be unhappy either way.

  • The "I want interim but pay fractional prices" trap: You hire a fractional CMO at $15K/month and expect them to be available daily for the next 6 months. They aren't; they have other clients. Either you've underbought (you need interim) or you've overscoped (you need fractional working at fractional cadence).
  • The "I want fractional but bought interim" trap: You hire an interim CMO at $30K/month for what you think is a permanent need. After 6 months you're spending $360K/year for someone working at maintenance mode rather than building. A fractional CMO at $180K/year would have built more.

The diagnostic: Ask yourself, "In 12 months, is there a permanent CMO in this seat?" If yes → interim. If you're not sure → fractional. If definitely not → fractional.

Hybrid models

When the line blurs

Some engagements legitimately combine both shapes. Examples:

  • Fractional CMO scaling up temporarily. A fractional CMO at $20K/month may agree to scale up to $35K/month for 90 days during a critical launch or transition, then step back down. This works when the underlying relationship is healthy and both sides understand the temporary shape.
  • Interim CMO converting to fractional. An interim CMO who lands at the right company sometimes converts the engagement to a longer fractional model once the immediate gap is bridged. Often a great outcome — the interim leader already knows the business deeply.
  • Fractional-to-full-time pipeline. A fractional CMO who builds the function over 12 months becomes the leading candidate for the permanent CMO role. Common path for some firms.
Not sure which role you actually need?
